title: Administrative centre

infobox.value: is a seat of regional administration or local government, or a [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") or [town](/wiki/County_town "County town"), or the place where the central administration of a [commune](/wiki/Township "Township") or equivalent subdivision, is located.

of a region, cercle or department is usually also a communal **chef-lieu**. Both nations also established a supreme council of local government authorities (*Haut conseil des collectivités territoriales*) seated at the nation's capital. Smaller subdivisions in Mali's communes (villages, tribal councils, and quarters) are administered from a *place* (lit. 'site'), so the *chef-lieu* is literally the 'chief-place' even at the lowest level.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Seat of regional administration or local government

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Evian-les-Bains_%28Haute-Savoie%29_%2810004827914%29.jpg/330px-Evian-les-Bains_%28Haute-Savoie%29_%2810004827914%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Evian-les-Bains_(Haute-Savoie)_(10004827914).jpg)

[Évian-les-Bains](/wiki/%C3%89vian-les-Bains "Évian-les-Bains"), administrative centre of the [canton of Évian-les-Bains](/wiki/Canton_of_%C3%89vian-les-Bains "Canton of Évian-les-Bains") in [Haute-Savoie](/wiki/Haute-Savoie "Haute-Savoie"), [France](/wiki/France "France")

An **administrative centre** is a seat of regional administration or local government, or a [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") or [town](/wiki/County_town "County town"), or the place where the central administration of a [commune](/wiki/Township "Township") or equivalent subdivision, is located.

In countries with French as the administrative language, such as Belgium, Luxembourg, Switzerland and many African countries, a ***chef-lieu*** (French pronunciation: [[ʃɛfljø]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"), pl. *chefs-lieux*, lit. 'chief place' or 'main place') is a town or city that is important from an administrative perspective.

## By country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: By country")]

### Algeria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Algeria")]

The capitals of [Algerian provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Algeria "Provinces of Algeria"), [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Algeria "Districts of Algeria"), and [communes](/wiki/Communes_of_Algeria "Communes of Algeria") are called *chefs-lieux*.

### Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Belgium")]

The *chef-lieu* in Belgium is the administrative centre of each of the ten [provinces of Belgium](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium"). Three of these cities also give their name to their province ([Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège") and [Namur](/wiki/Namur "Namur")).

### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: France")]

The *chef-lieu* of a French [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") is known as the [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_in_France "Prefectures in France") (*préfecture*). This is the town or city where the [prefect](/wiki/Prefect "Prefect") of the department (and all services under their control) are situated, in a building also known as the [prefecture](/wiki/Prefecture "Prefecture"). In every [French region](/wiki/French_region "French region"), one of the departments has preeminence over the others, and the prefect carries the title of *préfet de la région* (lit. 'Prefect of the Region') or *préfet du département* (lit. 'Prefect of the Department') and the city where the regional prefect is found is known as the *chef-lieu* of the region. The services are, however, controlled by the prefecture of the department.

The *chef-lieu* of an [arrondissement](/wiki/Arrondissements_of_France "Arrondissements of France"), commonly known as the *[sous-préfecture](/wiki/Subprefectures_in_France "Subprefectures in France")* is the city or town where the sub-prefect of the arrondissement (and the services directly under their control) are situated, in a building called the [sub-prefecture](/wiki/Sub-prefecture "Sub-prefecture"). The arrondissement where the department prefecture is located does not normally have a sub-prefect or sub-prefecture, the administration being devolved usually to the secretary-general of the departmental prefecture, who functions as sub-prefect for the arrondissement.

The *chef-lieu* of a [canton](/wiki/Cantons_of_France "Cantons of France") is usually the biggest city or town within the canton, but has only a nominal role. No specific services are controlled by it. In past decades, there was always a [gendarmerie](/wiki/Gendarmerie "Gendarmerie"), a treasurer and a justice of the peace.

#### New Caledonia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New Caledonia")]

The *chef-lieu* indicates the principal city of the provinces of New Caledonia. So [Nouméa](/wiki/Noum%C3%A9a "Nouméa") is the chef-lieu of South Province. But the chef-lieu can also mean the principal area within a town. So [Wé](/wiki/W%C3%A9 "Wé") is part of the town of [Lifou](/wiki/Lifou "Lifou"), but is the chef-lieu of Lifou. In the [Loyalty Islands](/wiki/Loyalty_Islands "Loyalty Islands") and the other islands, the name of the chef-lieu differs from that of the name of the town. For the towns of the mainland, the chef-lieu has the same name as the town. Nouméa is a town composed only of Nouméa.

### Francophone West Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Francophone West Africa")]

Many of the West African states which gained independence from [France](/wiki/France "France") in the mid-20th century also inherited the French administrative structure of departments and communes, headed by a *chef-lieu*. States still using *chef-lieu* to identify the administrative headquarters of a government subdivision include [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal"), [Burkina Faso](/wiki/Burkina_Faso "Burkina Faso"), [Benin](/wiki/Benin "Benin"), [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali"), and [Niger](/wiki/Niger "Niger").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Taking [Niger](/wiki/Communes_of_Niger "Communes of Niger") and [Mali](/wiki/Communes_of_Mali "Communes of Mali") as examples, the administrative subdivisions down to the commune level each have a formal place of administrative headquarters, titled the *chef-lieu*. The larger portion of the terminology of administrative division is inherited from colonial rule as part of [French West Africa](/wiki/French_West_Africa "French West Africa"), and has survived and been somewhat modified over time. In both nations there have been remarkably parallel histories.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Since decentralization began in both nations in the 1990s, the *chef-lieu* transitioned from the location of the governor, commandant, or prefect and their staff to the location of the commune, [cercle](/wiki/Cercles_of_Mali "Cercles of Mali"), [department](/wiki/Departments_of_Niger "Departments of Niger"), and regional councils and a variety of decentralized bodies.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The **chef-lieu** of a region, cercle or department is usually also a communal **chef-lieu**. Both nations also established a supreme council of local government authorities (*Haut conseil des collectivités territoriales*) seated at the nation's capital. Smaller subdivisions in Mali's communes (villages, tribal councils, and quarters) are administered from a *place* (lit. 'site'), so the *chef-lieu* is literally the 'chief-place' even at the lowest level.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Jordan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Jordan")]

In [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), the administrative centres are known as chief towns or [*nahias*](/wiki/Nahias_of_Jordan "Nahias of Jordan").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Nahias may be in charge of a sub-district (*qda*), a district (*liwa*), or a [governorate](/wiki/Governorates_of_Jordan "Governorates of Jordan") (*muhafazah*).

### Luxembourg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Luxembourg")]

Luxembourg is divided into four [electoral constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Luxembourg "Constituencies of Luxembourg"), twelve [cantons](/wiki/Cantons_of_Luxembourg "Cantons of Luxembourg"), as well as 100 [communes](/wiki/Communes_of_Luxembourg "Communes of Luxembourg") ([Luxembourgish](/wiki/Luxembourgish_language "Luxembourgish language"): *Gemengen*). Cantons have each a *chef-lieu* and are named after it. The same is true for each commune which is composed of more than one town or village. Usually, with a few exceptions, the commune is named after the communal *chef-lieu*.

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Russia")]

In [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), several million-plus cities in [federal districts](/wiki/Federal_districts_of_Russia "Federal districts of Russia") have the official status of an administrative centre: [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") (as the main city of the [Central Federal District](/wiki/Central_Federal_District "Central Federal District")), [Vladivostok](/wiki/Vladivostok "Vladivostok"), [Volgograd](/wiki/Volgograd "Volgograd"), [Yekaterinburg](/wiki/Yekaterinburg "Yekaterinburg"), [Nizhny Novgorod](/wiki/Nizhny_Novgorod "Nizhny Novgorod"), [Novosibirsk](/wiki/Novosibirsk "Novosibirsk"), [Pyatigorsk](/wiki/Pyatigorsk "Pyatigorsk"), [Rostov-on-Don](/wiki/Rostov-on-Don "Rostov-on-Don") and [St. Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"). The main cities of regions and municipal districts are also called unofficially the administrative centre or simply the centre. The only exception to this rule is the [republics](/wiki/Republics_of_Russia "Republics of Russia"), for which the term "[capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city")" is used to refer to the seat of government. The capital of Russia is also an entity to which the term "administrative centre" does not apply.

### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sweden")]

In [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") there are two levels of administrative centre; the local [municipal](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Sweden "Municipalities of Sweden") and the regional [county](/wiki/Counties_of_Sweden "Counties of Sweden").

#### Central locality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Central locality")]

Central locality ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *centralort*) is a term commonly ascribed to the settlement that serves as a municipal administrative centre. This level handles the local administrative and political tasks of the surrounding settlements. Since [central place theory](/wiki/Central_place_theory "Central place theory") was the guiding principle during the 1962–1977 municipal reform, most municipalities were dominated by a larger urban area where the political seat was located. Most municipalities are named for their central locality, but there are several exceptions.

There are many deviations from the central locality principle. Some municipalities are dominated by two or more towns of similar size, and sometimes they share the municipal administration, with the municipality having its official address in one of the towns. For example, both [Skillingaryd](/wiki/Skillingaryd "Skillingaryd") and [Vaggeryd](/wiki/Vaggeryd "Vaggeryd") are central localities of [Vaggeryd Municipality](/wiki/Vaggeryd_Municipality "Vaggeryd Municipality"). Conversely, there are municipalities within [metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_Areas_of_Sweden "Metropolitan Areas of Sweden"). For example, there are twenty-six municipalities within the [Stockholm metropolitan area](/wiki/Stockholm_metropolitan_area "Stockholm metropolitan area"). The term "central locality" has no legal meaning and it is unclear how it should be applied to these municipalities. Some municipalities appointing one or several localities to be the central locality.

#### Residence city

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Residence city")]

A residence city ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *residensstad*) is the town or city which is the [political and administrative seat of the county](/wiki/County_administrative_boards_of_Sweden "County administrative boards of Sweden"). This level handles the more regional political and administrative tasks of the county, such as healthcare and [public transport](/wiki/Public_transport "Public transport"). The name comes from these towns or cities being where the governor (*landshövding*) resides.

### Switzerland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Switzerland")]

The term *chef-lieu* is applied to the capital of each [Swiss canton](/wiki/Swiss_canton "Swiss canton"). In 16 of the 26 cantons, the territory is subdivided into districts. Every district's capital is also termed a *chef-lieu* and each has a prefect.

### Tunisia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Tunisia")]

The term *chef-lieu* is used to designate the capital of each [governorate](/wiki/Governorates_of_Tunisia "Governorates of Tunisia") of [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia"). Each of the 24 governorates is subdivided into [delegations](/wiki/Delegations_of_Tunisia "Delegations of Tunisia") which each have a central city as the *chef-lieu* of the delegation.

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), an administrative centre is the centre of a [local authority](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_Kingdom "Local government in the United Kingdom"), which is distinct from the [county town](/wiki/County_town#Great_Britain,_historic "County town") of a [historic county](/wiki/Counties_of_the_United_Kingdom "Counties of the United Kingdom"). For example, the administrative centre where the county council of [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire") sits is Preston, even though the county town is Lancaster.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_centre&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

* *[The Fiancée of the pirate](/wiki/A_Very_Curious_Girl "A Very Curious Girl")* (1969) is a film by [Nelly Kaplan](/wiki/Nelly_Kaplan "Nelly Kaplan"), where the action takes place in a hamlet where everyone spends their time worrying about what everyone thinks about the chef-lieu of the town.

* [County town](/wiki/County_town "County town"), administrative centres in Ireland and Great Britain
